learning_objectives: Apply the Extended Euclidean Algorithm to find modular inverses
Understand prime numbers and their role in cryptographic systems
readings: Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications Ch. 4

lecture_notes.introduction: The mathematics underlying modern cryptography is primarily number theory. These concepts are not merely theoretical; they directly determine the security strength of the encryption systems that protect the internet.
key_concepts: Modular arithmetic: addition, subtraction, multiplication, exponentiation
Greatest Common Divisor and Extended Euclidean Algorithm
Modular inverse: a^(-1) mod n
Prime numbers and primality testing
Euler's totient function and Euler's theorem
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing the Extended Euclidean Algorithm in Python and using it to compute modular inverses for RSA key generation

review.value: Operations performed within a finite set {0, 1, ..., n-1}. Example: 17 mod 5 = 2. Fundamental to all public-key cryptography. RSA, Diffie-Hellman, and ECC all operate in modular arithmetic.
If gcd(a, n) = 1, then a^phi(n) mod n = 1. This is the mathematical foundation of RSA: because e*d = 1 mod phi(n), encrypting with e and decrypting with d returns the original message.

learning_objectives: Define and work with mathematical groups, rings, and fields
Understand finite fields (Galois Fields) used in AES
Apply group theory to elliptic curve cryptography

lecture_notes.introduction: Algebraic structures provide the mathematical framework for all modern cryptographic algorithms. AES operates in GF(2^8), and elliptic curve cryptography uses groups on elliptic curves.
key_concepts: Groups: closure, associativity, identity, inverse
Cyclic groups and generators
Finite fields GF(p) and GF(2^n)
GF(2^8) arithmetic used in AES (SubBytes, MixColumns)
Elliptic curve groups: point addition and scalar multiplication
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing GF(2^8) arithmetic in Python and performing an AES SubBytes operation manually

review.value: Galois Field with 256 elements. Addition is XOR. Multiplication uses polynomial arithmetic modulo an irreducible polynomial. AES performs all operations in this field.
Points on y^2 = x^3 + ax + b over a finite field form a group under point addition. The Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP) is the basis for ECC security.

class_schedule.topic: Probability and Information Theory for Security
learning_objectives: Apply probability to model security events and attack success rates
Understand entropy as a measure of randomness and key strength
Analyze the birthday paradox and its implications for hash collisions
readings: Information Theory, Inference, and Learning Algorithms Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Probability and information theory quantify security strength. How long until an attacker guesses a password? How many hashes before a collision? These questions are answered by probability.
key_concepts: Probability distributions for security analysis
Entropy: H(X) = -sum(p(x) * log2(p(x)))
Password entropy calculation and strength assessment
Birthday paradox: collision probability grows quadratically
Birthday attack on hash functions: 2^(n/2) operations for n-bit hash
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating the entropy of various password policies, then demonstrating the birthday paradox through a simulation and applying it to hash collision probability

review.value: Entropy = log2(possible_combinations). 8 lowercase letters: log2(26^8) = 37.6 bits. 12 mixed-case with numbers and symbols: log2(95^12) = 78.8 bits. More entropy = harder to brute force.

For a 256-bit hash, brute force pre-image requires 2^256 operations. But finding ANY collision (two inputs with same hash) requires only 2^128 operations due to the birthday paradox. This is why hash output must be 2x the desired security level.

learning_objectives: Distinguish between P, NP, and NP-hard problems
Understand the hardness assumptions underlying cryptographic systems
Evaluate the impact of algorithmic advances on cryptographic security
readings: Introduction to Algorithms: Complexity Theory chapter

lecture_notes.introduction: Cryptographic security is based on the assumption that certain mathematical problems are computationally infeasible to solve. Understanding these assumptions reveals both the strengths and the vulnerabilities of our security systems.
key_concepts: P vs NP: polynomial vs non-deterministic polynomial time
One-way functions: easy to compute, hard to invert
Integer factorization: the hardness assumption behind RSA
Discrete logarithm: the hardness assumption behind Diffie-Hellman and ECC
Lattice problems: the hardness assumption behind post-quantum cryptography
lecture_notes.demonstration: Timing RSA key generation for increasing key sizes to observe the relationship between key size and computational cost, then comparing with elliptic curve key generation

review.value: Given n = p*q where p and q are large primes, finding p and q from n is computationally infeasible for sufficiently large n. RSA-2048 factorization is estimated to require 2^112 operations.

Shortest Vector Problem (SVP) and Learning With Errors (LWE) are believed to be hard even for quantum computers. This is why lattice-based cryptography (CRYSTALS-Kyber/Dilithium) is the basis for post-quantum standards.

learning_objectives: Apply statistical methods to network anomaly detection
Use hypothesis testing for security event analysis
Build statistical baselines for behavioral analytics
readings: Statistics for Machine Learning: relevant chapters

lecture_notes.introduction: Security analytics relies on statistics to distinguish normal from abnormal behavior. Statistical methods underpin IDS, UEBA, and threat hunting.
key_concepts: Descriptive statistics: mean, median, standard deviation for baseline building
Hypothesis testing: is this network behavior normal or anomalous?
Type I (false positive) and Type II (false negative) errors in security
Bayesian inference: updating threat probability with new evidence
Time series analysis for detecting beaconing and periodic C2
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a statistical baseline of network traffic and using z-scores to detect anomalous spikes indicative of data exfiltration

review.value: z = (x - mean) / std_dev. A value with z > 3 is more than 3 standard deviations from the mean, occurring with <0.3% probability. Used to flag anomalous traffic volumes, connection counts, or data transfer sizes.

Lowering the detection threshold catches more attacks (fewer false negatives) but generates more false alerts (more false positives). The ROC curve helps find the optimal balance for a given environment.

class_schedule.topic: Linear Algebra and Machine Learning Foundations for Security
learning_objectives: Apply matrix operations to security data analysis
Understand dimensionality reduction for security feature analysis
Connect mathematical foundations to ML-based security tools
readings: Mathematics for Machine Learning: Linear Algebra chapters

lecture_notes.introduction: Machine learning security tools are built on linear algebra and optimization. Understanding these foundations enables you to evaluate, tune, and trust ML-based security tools.
key_concepts: Vectors and matrices for representing security data (feature vectors)
Matrix multiplication for data transformation
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for dimensionality reduction
Distance metrics: Euclidean, cosine similarity for anomaly detection
Gradient descent: how ML models learn from security data
lecture_notes.demonstration: Using PCA to reduce a high-dimensional network traffic dataset to visualizable dimensions, revealing clusters of normal and anomalous traffic

review.value: Each network flow is represented as a vector of features: [packet_count, byte_count, duration, avg_packet_size, ...]. ML algorithms operate on these vectors to classify flows as normal or malicious.
Reduces high-dimensional security data (50+ features) to 2-3 dimensions for visualization. Anomalies that are invisible in individual features often become clearly separated in PCA-reduced space.

questions.question: In RSA, n = p * q where p = 61 and q = 53. Calculate: (a) n, (b) phi(n), (c) a valid public exponent e that is coprime to phi(n), and (d) the corresponding private exponent d such that e * d mod phi(n) = 1.

rubric.key: Correct d via Extended Euclidean Algorithm

questions.question: A company's password policy requires 8-character passwords using lowercase letters (26), uppercase letters (26), digits (10), and special characters (10). Calculate the entropy of a randomly generated password under this policy. How many passwords per second would an attacker need to try to exhaust the keyspace in one year? Compare this to bcrypt at 4 hashes/second.
questions.answer: Character set size = 26 + 26 + 10 + 10 = 72. Possible passwords = 72^8 = 722,204,136,308,736 (approximately 7.22 * 10^14). Entropy = log2(72^8) = 8 * log2(72) = 8 * 6.17 = 49.4 bits. To exhaust in one year (31,536,000 seconds): 7.22 * 10^14 / 3.15 * 10^7 = 22,912,147 passwords per second. Modern GPUs achieve 10+ billion SHA-256 hashes/second, so SHA-256-hashed passwords are cracked in under a minute. With bcrypt at 4 hashes/second: 7.22 * 10^14 / 4 = 1.8 * 10^14 seconds = approximately 5.7 million years. bcrypt makes brute force infeasible even for this moderate entropy password.

rubric.key: Correct throughput calculation for one year

quizzes.topic: Complexity Theory, Statistics, and ML Foundations

questions.question: A network IDS uses z-score anomaly detection. The baseline shows average daily outbound data transfer of 500 MB with a standard deviation of 100 MB. Today's transfer is 950 MB. Calculate the z-score and explain: at a threshold of z > 3, would this trigger an alert? What is the statistical significance of this finding?
questions.answer: z = (950 - 500) / 100 = 4.5. Since z = 4.5 > 3 (threshold), yes, this triggers an alert. Statistical significance: assuming normal distribution, a z-score of 4.5 corresponds to a probability of approximately 3.4 * 10^(-6), meaning this level of traffic occurs by chance roughly once every 294,000 days (about 800 years). This is highly anomalous and warrants investigation. However, z-score detection assumes normal distribution. If the actual distribution is heavy-tailed (common in network traffic), the threshold should be adjusted. Also consider day-of-week patterns (end-of-month reporting might cause legitimate spikes). Context and domain knowledge must supplement statistical detection.

questions.question: Explain why Shor's quantum algorithm threatens RSA but not AES, referencing the specific mathematical problems each relies on and the impact of quantum computing on each.
questions.answer: RSA relies on the hardness of integer factorization: given n = p*q, finding p and q. Shor's algorithm solves integer factorization in polynomial time O((log n)^3) on a quantum computer, completely breaking RSA regardless of key size. RSA-2048 would be factored in hours on a sufficiently large quantum computer. AES is a symmetric cipher that does not rely on a mathematical hard problem in the same way. Its security comes from the difficulty of searching through the keyspace (brute force). Grover's quantum algorithm provides a quadratic speedup for unstructured search: AES-256's keyspace of 2^256 is reduced to 2^128 quantum operations. Since 2^128 operations remain computationally infeasible, AES-256 is considered quantum-safe. AES-128 would be reduced to 2^64 operations, which may become feasible, so AES-256 is recommended for post-quantum security. This fundamental difference is why post-quantum research focuses on replacing RSA/ECC (asymmetric) while keeping AES (symmetric).

rubric.key: Correct explanation of Shor's impact on RSA
Correct explanation of Grover's impact on AES

major_assignments.title: Applied Cryptographic Mathematics Implementation
major_assignments.description: Implement core mathematical algorithms used in cryptography from scratch (no crypto library functions for the core algorithms). Analyze their security properties mathematically.
objectives: Implement RSA key generation, encryption, and decryption from mathematical primitives
Analyze the mathematical security properties of each implementation
deliverables: Python implementations of RSA and Diffie-Hellman
Written proofs of key mathematical theorems used

criteria: RSA key generation with proper prime selection
Diffie-Hellman key exchange produces shared secret
Security strength analysis for each implementation
Relationship between key size and security level
Proof that RSA decryption recovers plaintext
Birthday bound derivation for hash collisions
Correct implementation of cryptographic algorithms from primitives
Rigorous mathematical analysis of security properties
Clear mathematical proofs demonstrating deep understanding

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of mathematical concepts and computational skills

midterm_examination.description: Assessment covering number theory, algebra, and probability

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all applied mathematics topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all class sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Problem sets may be discussed. All proofs and implementations must be individually written.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Mathematical proofs must be your own work. Using computer algebra systems for computation is permitted; using them to generate proofs is not.
course_policies.calculator_policy: Scientific calculators permitted during exams. Programmable calculators and computers are not permitted during exams.
